speaker: interviewer
text: This is roll one with Peter Daniels. Roll one, Peter Daniels. Give me a slate there also. I can cheat there before [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: I just hope nobody asks me if that's a banana in my pocket. Today-- April 11th, 1995. The testimony of Peter Daniels interviewed by Mark Rothman in Los Angeles, California, United States of America. The language will be English.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Mark Rothman. I'd like to make a correction to the slate. It's April 12, 1995. We're in Los Angeles. I'm with Peter Daniels. We're speaking English. Good morning, Mr. Daniels. Thank you very much for speaking with us this morning. Why don't you start by introducing yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Peter Daniels, Heinz Daniels.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you could spell your name

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The last name is D-A-N-I-E-L-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your first and middle names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Peter Heinz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And spell them please.

speaker: interviewee
text: P-E-T-E-R H-E-I-N-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you begin by telling us where you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: Berlin, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when?

speaker: interviewee
text: July 8th, 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us anything about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was raised in a-- in a mixed Jewish home. Her father was Jewish. Her mother was non-Jewish. And my father was raised in a Jewish home. His family was a-- his father was a legal-- was an attorney in Germany. And my father was-- I don't think he started his profession at the time he became my father. They were both in their mid 20s.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name is Eric, E-R-I-C Daniels. And my mother's name was Hilde Berlowitz, B-E-R-L-O-W-I-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how is Hilde spelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-I-L-D-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know anything about how they came together or what their life like was together?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a brief romance, if truth be told. It wasn't a long relationship. And it was a-- I would like to think I was a love child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother talk about that all as you were growing up?

words.text: all as you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard for her or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, it took many, many years before my mother would even allow that information. And at that point, it was a lot of prodding. And that's about as much as I know about that affair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know very much about your mother's life before you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, her father came from the Polish border. His father was a schoolteacher. They wanted to send their son, who was my mother's father, to a larger city. So they sent him to Berlin in his early youth. It was probably about 1870, 1880. He went to work for a steel metal manufacturer, became like a personal assistant. And he did actually quite well. He was a founder of the Welle-Poseidon Rowing Club in Berlin on the Wannsee, where the Wannsee Conference took place. And that club was founded in 1895, I believe. 1890 or '95. He was one of the co-founders. And so he became somewhat of a fixture in the Jewish community. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And for the life of me, I do not remember his first-- first name. His last time, of course, was Berlowitz. But I do not remember his first name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have much contact with him as a little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember him. He probably knew me. He died in 1941, so I was five years old. And because of his somewhat status in Berlin, he was-- he was able to stay out the-- being deported to the camps. And-- but he did die in 1941 so-- in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he murdered by the Nazis, or did he die--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think he-- yeah, he died of natural causes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of early childhood do you remember having?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my childhood was not very pleasant because I was born in 1936. And by the time I was probably ready to go to school, which was 1939, 1940, the schools were already shut out for us. So I never did attend any public schools. And it was my mother and I. She-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was no father in the house. In fact, his family and he went to Shanghai with a very large group of Germans that migrated to Shanghai in the late '30s. So I never saw him. I don't even know if he knew who I was. And actually, I never even saw a picture of him. I have never-- I have no idea what the man even looks like. I'm sure he's not even alive anymore. But we lived in an-- in an apartment in Berlin. And my mother had to go out and work She had to try to find work. She was a seamstress and dressmaker. And you know, attempted to find work. She would leave me alone all day long in an apartment when I was three, four, five. I-- she had no choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: No one came in to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody came in. I mean, the Germans were never much for babysitters anyway. It was just never much of a tradition to have a babysitter. I guess you just learned to make do early on. And of course, in those days, she couldn't afford a babysitter and who else would be a babysitter for Jewish kid anyway? So it just-- she just basically trained me early on to fend for myself. She would have whatever food there was available sitting there. She told me what time to eat, what time to go to the bathroom, never to leave the house. Gave me lessons, gave me-- you know, she prepared some lessons for me, so she basically tutored me at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I lived out my early years pretty much as a-- very much alone and essentially locked up in an apartment, because I was not allowed to leave the house by myself. What would you do all day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't really remember specifically.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd sit in front of the window, and look out the window a lot. And I-- I guess I found ways of amusing myself. And of course, I had studies. My mother would give me some lessons that I had to do. And those had to be completed by the time she came home. I had to-- I guess I was reading or whatever else I would do. But I spent a lot of time by myself. But I don't remember specifically.

speaker: interviewer
text: One thing you mentioned is that you were told what time to eat. Do you remember learning to tell time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember learning to tell time. I probably just-- it was probably drummed into me very early on. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I suppose if I didn't and I ate too early, I was hungry all day. I learned for the next day. So I don't really recall learning time. But she had put me on a regular-- on a-- on a schedule that I had to follow. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember having cousins, or nephews? You know, uncles and aunts?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I think there were a couple of small-- there were a couple of relatives. I think she-- my mother had a relative. She-- I don't know if she had a cousin that lived outside of Berlin who lived in small suburbs of Berlin that we might have occasionally visited. I remember traveling trains frequently-- you know, occasionally to visit somebody out in the country. But it was so rare that it wasn't, you know-- it wasn't very prevalent in my mind. There's just some vague recollections.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother born in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she was born in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: After your grandfather had come from Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And she hadn't-- did she have friends? Do you remember if she spent time with friends at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't remember. I don't remember. I don't remember too many people in my life outside my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any kind of Jewish life?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No Jewish life whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of yourself as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- I-- I knew I was Jewish, because we had to wear a Jewish star. From the time I remember, we had a wear a Jewish star when we left the house on all of our clothing, so I knew that concept. But as far as religious training or even cultural training as a Jew, we never got it. I knew I was Jewish, and I knew that there were a lot of restrictions on me. In that I couldn't leave the house without the star. And I knew that I couldn't go to school. And I knew that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, of those limitations because I was Jewish. Although I-- nobody ever sat me down and gave me a history-- a lesson in Judaism. So I was not-- not able to incorporate that or internalize that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you don't seem to have had the opportunity to make any friends with other-- I guess only Jewish kids. But did you have any childhood friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. If I did, I-- they were very fleeting. I don't-- I wouldn't recall them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember celebrating any of your early birthdays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember-- I remember one. Interesting that you ask. It wasn't a kinder-- it wasn't a-- I don't know if it was my fourth or fifth birthday. My sixth birthday, I may have-- fifth or sixth, I think. The seventh, I was already in a camp. It was at a kinderheim or some kind of a nursery school or preschool that my mother was able to get me into for a short period of time. And I remember it was my birthday one day and while I was there. And I remember that I got into a confrontation with another child. And it was probably because he called me a dirty Jew or something. I mean, most of the time, those were the only things that would trigger me off. And I remember throwing a pair of scissors. And the scissor actually cut the kid. It wasn't-- it wasn't life threatening. It just cut him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they locked me in a closet. That's how they handled things. They just locked me in a closet. And then my mother came with a birthday cake. And they all had the birthday cake while I was sitting in a closet watching them eat birthday cake. So it was a lesson. [LAUGHS] And that was a birthday I remember. A painful lesson.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- yeah, it was a painful lesson. And anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, while we were living in the apartment, the main thing my mother always admonished me is never open up the door to anybody. Absolutely never open up the door to anybody. And so one day, there was a knock on the door, and I was able to get through the peephole. And I saw two or three men in white-- in-- in businesses suits. Of course, I opened up the door like I shouldn't have. And they were the SS. And they came and asked where my mother was. I don't know, she's not-- and she'll be home soon. So they waited. They were very polite. They waited. I don't recall how long it was, a few hours. But when she came home, I do remember seeing her. And she knew immediately who they were. And I don't think we were home five minutes. They just-- she just came in, grabbed the bags, locked the door, and they took us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the bags packed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No bags packed, nothing. They just closed the apartment. All she was able to grab was her purse, and off we went. You want me to go on in the sequence of events?

speaker: interviewer
text: This is very interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So, well, we went to the local train station where we were gathered with hundreds-- I'm sure it wasn't thousands, but it was many, many other Jews. And we were ready to be transported. At that point, I had no idea where we were going, but it was at the local train station. And I don't know if we were there for days. It seems like it. I remember hanging around, and-- and sitting, and sleeping. I could've been there 24 hours, 30 hours. There was no food. We were just all hanging around. And then I was feeling-- I wasn't feeling very well. And I was becoming-- getting ill. And we were all hanging around. And at one point, a couple of German-- excuse me-- officers in a uniform came by. And they noticed that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was ill. And one of them recognized that I had, at that point, found out later that I had jaundice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he immediately had ordered me taken away, sent to the local hospital for treatment. And whatever treatment they had at that point-- I don't know if they even had any vaccination. But they actually isolated me in a-- in a hospital unit, and I was able to recuperate there for about a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: This gave my mother a chance to go back to the apartment, straighten out whatever affairs-- I think she was able to pack some-- some of her personal belongings. She may be able hide a few things. I don't know. And she did visit me once or twice in the hospital. And I must have been there a week, maybe 10 days. And then when I recuperated from jaundice, then we were then shipped out. We went to another one of these things with the train stations. I don't remember. That is, I don't remember that time frame very well. At that point, we may have been loaded onto the-- to the trains almost immediately, I think. I found out a lot of years later-- in fact, it was my mother who told me, probably only a few years later that that German officer who had separated me out from the rest and ordered me to the hospital was Adolf Eichmann. He was at that point-- he was in charge of that-- that was his job at that time. He was gathering all of the people in Germany for-- for transporting.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet he showed you some kindness.

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- well, I don't know if he showed me kindness. Maybe he didn't want an epidemic right there in the heart of Germany, in Berlin. [LAUGHS] Because we were headed for Thereseinstadt. And at that point, Theresienstadt was not-- in '43, was not-- was not considered to be a death camp or a-- you know, it was considered to be sort of like a way station, the model camp. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard anything about it? Did you have any idea where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I had no idea. We didn't know. I didn't know where I was going. My mother may have known, but I was not aware. I may have been aware, but it didn't register to me. But this was at a train station in Berlin. That's the last thing he wanted was probably he didn't want an outbreak of jaundice, or yellow fever, or whatever else may have occurred right then and there. They would have had a mess on their hands. So whether it was kindness or it was just expeditious for him just to, you know, get rid of that source immediately, and just take care of that, and move everybody out. I think it was probably-- from his organized mind that was his thinking. So I--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you treated in the hospital? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall. It wasn't bad. I don't remember. It wasn't-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think I was just probably just another patient there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember missing your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember. I might have, but I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then we went to-- then we were-- we were loaded onto the-- when I left there, we were loaded onto the trains. They were not Amtrak, I can tell you this. [LAUGHS] I didn't truly know that all those cattle cars that you see in the-- in the Holocaust museums were truly the ones. And I do remember being in one of those, and they were just disgusting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I think I was. I think I was with my mother. But we were all standing or sitting. There were-- there were not seats. I mean, they were basically just cattle cars. And we were headed to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given any food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bathroom facilities-- do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they probably stopped the cars once or twice for us to get out, where we had to go to the bathroom, and then we were loaded back on. I don't recall, but I would think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how long you were on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't recall. But I figured probably it couldn't have taken more than 24 hours, maybe 30 hours. I don't know. I mean, from Berlin to Prague. It's not that great of a distance. You know, the train is moving on pretty rapidly.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, a question I've always wondered about is could you tell it was night or day in the car?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall. It seemed like it was always night. It seemed like it was always night. I know that when we got there, it was night. It was cold. It was in January of '43. It was in the middle of the night. I know that we got out of the car. I remember we getting out and walking en masse to the barracks in Theresienstadt. I don't know how long we walked. It seemed like we walked a long time, maybe an hour. Maybe-- I don't-- you know, I'm just thinking I was six years old. So-- and we walked. And then they-- we had no idea where we were going. And they just herded us into-- into some barracks just to-- to get rid of us, I guess. Just to put us someplace until they had a chance to organize us. And I remember sleeping on a cold floor. I remember there were rats. I well remember hearing noises. I guess, it must have been in an attic or some kind of a large hallway-- hall that we were sleeping in. There were no lights. I remember the first night. It was really kind of scary. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then-- I don't remember the sequence of the events after that. I was separated from my mother. I was put into a children's compound within the camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My head was shaven. The children's compound-- I-- I never did understand why they separated us out. I guess probably for expeditiously-- that way they could control the parents. Maybe the parents wouldn't try any funny business if their children were separated from them. And I don't know how many children were there at-- at one time. But it was a separate barrack. And there could've been 50, 60, 75 children. They are just faces. I don't remember any one in particular. I don't think I recall any one or two children that I knew for any length of time. I think they just sort of came and went. And that's basically where we were housed. I saw my mother. I was supposed to see her. I mean, you know, they made it a point for me to visit with my mother once a week. Of whatever time I saw her, I think they were really kind of uneventful. I don't recall. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't recall them being very loving or emotional. I think it was just sort of duty bound. So I didn't really see her very often while we were in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's still amazingly humane for the world of the camps that they allowed you have any contact with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I suppose. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just when you look at it in terms of how children were treated at other camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all I'm saying.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in that sense, yeah, absolutely. I will agree that in retrospect, we were very lucky to have been in that camp. We were-- I don't really know what the-- what the goals were. I don't-- I never did find out what they had in mind for us. I know one of the reasons that we were in a camp is because my mother was able to-- to-- to convince the authority. It took a while-- that she was not 100% Jewish because of her mother was non-Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was able to get some paperwork out. And I don't think they were entirely convinced. But during the time that they were researching, the fact that she had some-- God forbid, Aryan blood in her-- but it was non-Jewish blood. And I really don't know what religion her mother was. I have no idea. She wasn't-- I don't even know what her relationship with her real mother was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But during that time, the authorities were just saying, well, we'll just keep you here until we make a determination whether we should send you on to other camps. So we basically stayed in Theresienstadt until the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now do you remember what day to day life was like? Did you go to school there or just work?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had lessons. We had-- there were some-- there was some semblance of order. There was a-- there was some women. There was some Jewish women who were designated as being our-- I don't know the right word. I don't think it was nursemaids. They were just sort of in charge of us. I believe they tried to tutor us. I think they tried giving us some lessons. I don't really recall. I did have some freedom. I did have some ability to be off on my own. I don't think they kept very close track of us there, the children. Or if they did, it's not like I was gone for days, but I was able to slip away from the-- from the regiment during the day for some time and wander off my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was-- I was somewhat of a loner. And I was able to explore some of the areas of the camp. And I was able to snoop around a lot of different areas. I don't recall a lot of things, but I remember being alone a lot. And not being lonely, but just being alone. I was sort of always sort of exploring a little bit. Where would you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just within the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- I mean, did you have a favorite place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably. I think I remember being on top of a little hill, on top of a knoll, and just sort of looking down at the barracks. Theresienstadt was a-- it was a sort of, like it was a barracks. It was a soldiers' barracks at one time, like a garrison. So it had barracks, and it had administrative buildings. And then of course, it had some open spaces-- green spaces they call them today. And so I was able to-- I would not go into buildings, but I would just-- I don't recall exactly. I just remember just, you know, exploring a lot on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did any Commanders or SS guards ever find you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably. But I don't remember being treated harshly personally, as far as physical harm. I remember we didn't very much. We had barley soup. I was raised on barley soup, and I love barley soup today. I just was-- that's all. Barley soup, 20 times a day. Well, one time a day, I guess. It's not very much else. Couple of instances, things I remember, I know that they used to bring a food supply into the camp on some of the trains. And we used to-- a few times, we would sneak out in the middle the night. In the dark of night-- kids-- and we would find our way to the railroads. And we would crawl underneath the railroad cars and look for small potatoes that used to fall in between the cracks. If they were small enough, they would fall between the cracks. And so we would gather up some potatoes and bring it back to the barracks with us. And we would slice them up. And we would lay them out on top of the stove. We had our own way of making french fries, I guess. That was the highlight of some of our days or nights, because those trains would come in maybe once a month or something like that. And we always knew there were a few potatoes that would-- that would fall. And we-- I'm sorry. Did you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You mentioned that there were certain-- not nurse maids, not really kapos it sounds like. Were they very cruel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't recall. I don't recall them being cruel. I don't recall them being loving.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they try and impose any kind of discipline?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall. I don't recall them being harsh. I don't recall them being-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like me to shut that dog up? Oh, I'm not sure we're hearing it on tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- they're vague. They're just vague recollections of mine. They were not very large. Or they didn't make a very large impression on me, so I don't recall them with any clarity. I don't remember them being cruel or kind. I think they were just-- it was really very neutral to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they change? Was there any consistency?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall. No, I don't recall. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you mentioned that you were finding potatoes. Were there other things like that that you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember we were in a work-- we, the children, were in work parties. And there was an interesting incident that I had recalled a number of years ago. And the few times I had related this incident, the interviewer I was talking to at that time had never heard of that type of an incident from any of the other children that she interviewed. And so it was always something that I thought, well, was I dreaming it and all that. And then-- I'll get to the incident in a minute. They interviewed somebody else on the East Coast for the same archives.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the Yale Archives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And it was amazing because it was a little girl who related that same incident. I-- it was completely independent of mine-- two or three years later. So they realized over time that it actually happened, but very few kids remembered it. And it was that we were loading up. We were a human chain-gang. We were loading up boxes. I think we were loading them on cars rather than unloading them. And they were just sort of like white boxes. We had no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you say a chain-gang, you mean passing the box from one--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Passing the boxes from one child to the next. From one of the warehouses going up right into the cars. And we really didn't know what they were until one of those boxes dropped, and fell, and opened up. And it was a human skull. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and I had talked about that. I had never heard of anybody else remembering that until that-- an independent corroboration occurred on the East Coast. And it must have been another child--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who saw the same box.

speaker: interviewee
text: --who was in that same work party as I was on that same day.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you react when that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall. It was probably pretty scary. It was probably--
